license_note: Confirmed via CrossRef DOI metadata: dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.y8ifzue
title: Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) for the Analysis of α-Cypermethrin in Biological Samples by Gas Chromatography with Electron Capture Detection
author: Ram Dhakal

steps_raw.instruction: Spike 100 µL of 1 µg/mL of cis/trans-permethrin in acetonitrile (100 ng each per sample) to every tube, including the tube with the Reference Standard.
Spike 100 L of 1 g/mL α-cypermethrin in acetonitrile (100 ng per sample) to the three OPR tubes and the Reference Standard. (Note: Do not spike blanks or the experimental samples)

Add 1.9 mL of acetonitrile to the method blanks and 1.8 mL of acetonitrile into the Reference Standard, OPR tubes, and experimental samples. ( Note: The total amount of acetonitrile in a sample should be 2 mL including the solvent of standard solutions)
Add 1 mL of Milli-Q water (20 m) into the tube.
Cap and vortex each sample for 1 min.
Centrifuge at 3,000 rpm (1811 x g) for 5 min to facilitate the phase separation.
Transfer 1 mL of the acetonitrile phase (top layer) to a dSPE tube (150 mg MgSO 4 /50 mg CEC 18, UCT, CUMC182CT). (Note: Make sure that no solids are transferred during this step)
Transfer 500 µL of each sample to the corresponding prelabeled GC vials and store capped vials at ambient temperature overnight.
Evaporate samples to dryness under a gentle stream of nitrogen.

Add 200 µL of 0.125 µL/mL deltamethrin in ethyl acetate (internal standard) and 800 µL ethyl acetate to all GC vial (25 ng per sample). (Note: other solvents, such as isooctane and acetonitrile , are not suitable solvents at this stage of the analysis)

An ethyl acetate solvent blank and the samples generated are analyzed on and Agilent GC with a μECD using the following temperature program: 50 °C, hold for 1 min, 15 °/min to 220 °C, 1 °/min to 240 °C, hold for 12 min, 15 °/min to 280 °C, and hold for 10 min. Helium (carrier gas) flow rate :1.0 mL/min. Inlet temperature: 240 °C. Detector temperature: 300 °C. Make-up flow: 60 mL/min.

Label disposable culture tubes and GC vials. Tubes and vials that need to be labeled include quality assurance/quality control (QA/QC) samples (i.e., water and matrix blank samples), experimental samples and the Reference Standard. Important: Use unique sample numbers that include your initials and identifiers for the experiment (e.g., notebook number) and the sample. The goal is to avoid identical sample numbers across experiments.
At least one tube for method blanks (water only).
At least one tube for sample blanks (matrix sample from unexposed or vehicle-exposed animals).
Three tubes for Ongoing Precision and Recovery (OPR) samples (matrix samples from unexposed or vehicle-exposed animals spiked with known amounts of the target analyte(s)).
Tubes for all samples (depends on number of samples to be analyzed; it is recommended that 20% of the samples are QA/QC samples; increase the number of blank samples accordingly).

One tube for the Reference Standard (will include known amounts of all analytes, including the surrogate recovery standards and internal standard) (Note: Solutions of all analytes are added to the tube with the Reference Standard at the same time when the samples are spiked)
Prepare the samples by adding the following to the prelabeled tubes:
Method blanks: Add 200 μL of water to the method blank tubes (each experiment needs to include at least one method blank).
Sample blanks: Add 200 mg of clean sample matrix (from unexposed or vehicle-exposed animals).
OPR: Add 200 mg of clean sample matrix the each of the three OPR tubes (from unexposed or vehicle-exposed animals).
Experimental samples from exposed animals: Add 200 mg of each samples to the corresponding vial (from exposed animals).
Sample quantification:

Integrate all chromatograms from the experiment, including the reference standard, using ChemStation software and export the data from the respective QUANTTAB.CSV file into the MS Excel document ( Example quantification of cypermethrin.xlsx ) attached to this protocol for quantification. Create additional worksheets for additional experimental samples or QA/QC standards as needed. Key formulas for the calculation of the relative retention time [RRT], relative response factor [RRF], the mass of the analyte in the sample, and the recovery of the surrogate standard [SURR recovery] are shown in the next step. (Note: The MS Excel document does not correct for the recovery of the surrogate recovery standard [SURR recovery])

Formulae for analysis: Formula 1, Relative retention time (RRT) Formula 2, Relative Response Factor Formula 3, Calculation of the mass [ng] of an analyte in a sample Formula 4, Recovery of the surrogate recovery standard (SURR recovery) added to a sample. If appropriate, the mass of the target analyte can be adjusted for the SURR recovery.
Cap, vortex for 1 min, and centrifuge at 3,000 rpm (1811 x g) for 5 mins.
